body:
The `updateLibrary` is a high-level API, same as `updateScene`, which also takes `appState` (which can alter UI state) and `commitToHistory`. It's about usefulness and semantics. Since opening the library menu is currently tied to updating the library, it makes sense to expose it.

The alternative is to force host apps to do an extra:

```
excalidrawAPI.updateScene({ appState: { isLibraryOpen: true } });
```

I don't care much, but I think the argument here and in your other comments stems from wanting to make this API low-level, which we don't need to.
author: dwelle

body:
We have a problem with grapheme clusters or multi-codepoint-letters or whatever you want to call them. The core issue with the current code is that  `[...'🤌🏼👌🏿']` evaluates to `[ '🤌', '🏼', '👌', '🏿' ]` 

https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/9257001/167246490-05ecd607-74e9-4d6d-ab9a-741cbb0d01a6.mov
author: tom-sherman

body:
I'm assuming that the text selection API also works on graphemes ie. selecting `🤌🏼` gives you a range of length 1 not 2. If this is the case I think the only solution is to have both `textElement.colorRanges` and `getLineGroupedRanges` work off of graphemes too.

In any case somewhere is going to have to be responsible for splitting by grapheme which probably means we need to pull in https://github.com/orling/grapheme-splitter

Edit: Text selection doesn't work on graphemes. Selecting any standard emoji gives you a range of length 2.
author: tom-sherman

body:
Ah, we should use https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/JavaScript/Reference/Global_Objects/Intl/Segmenter

Would need to include a polyfill for firefox tho, not sure on the strategy of supporty/polyfills in this repo. Can anyone comment?
author: tom-sherman

body:
haven't reviewed yet so unclear what issues you're facing exactly, but do we even need to be aware of graphemes? Can't we just save the exact offsets of a given selection range? I assume the answer is we can't mainly due to how it would work alongside line breaks when bound text comes into play?

If not, then the key is not to use array spread `[...string]` because it splits on code points (thus is potentially multi-byte), and instead use `string.split('')` which splits on bytes — then you can just index into the string as normal.
author: dwelle

body: The problem with doing `.split('')` here is that once they're split it's not clear how to recombine the codepoints to form the original graphemes. This is also why we need to be aware of graphemes in the first place, not sure you can apply a colour range without worrying about them because a range applies to graphemes - not codepoints.
author: tom-sherman

body:
Depending on what you do afterwards, you should still be able to treat the string as a black box and it won't really matter?

For example, if you have `Peru 🇵🇪`, and the user selected the flag emoji, the range would be `[5, 9]` (the flag is 4 bytes). You split which gets you:

```
const bytes = ['P', 'e', 'r', 'u', ' ', '\uD83C', '\uDDF5', '\uD83C', '\uDDEA']
```

You extract the flag by slicing it out:

```
const selection = bytes.slice(5, 9);
```

You can then `selection.join('')` which gets you the 🇵🇪 grapheme back.
author: dwelle

body:
this function must be optimized as it can take 150+ ms per frame for 50-point line (common in libraries) when called in the pointermove handler to determine which (mid)point you're hovering over — which causes noticeable lag, even race conditions where points tend to stay in hover state even after you move the cursor away.

If we can't make the algo better, e.g. narrow down to the segment the cursor is in instead of computing cursor distance to every line segment center, then we'll need to precalculate all segment midpoints and cache on line editor construction (it would also need to update on point move/delete/add/etc.).

body:
I agree with both of you. We definitely need to fix this issue. But having two props for theme (`props.theme` and `props.defaultTheme`) is not great, even though `defaultTheme` does solve this problem.

What we want is the ability to have the theme controlled (`props.theme`) but also show the UI toggle, _and_ still have the DX ok-ish.

So I suggest we keep the `props.theme` behavior as is, but make the (already existing) `props.UIOptions.canvasOptions.theme` to work in this way: default to `undefined` which means we'll render the theme toggle if `props.theme` is `undefined`. If `false`, never render it. If `true`, always render it regardless of `props.theme` existence.

We'll also want to factor out the theme persistence from `appState.theme` (`STORAGE_KEYS.LOCAL_STORAGE_APP_STATE`) to `theme` (`STORAGE_KEYS.THEME`). Not strictly necessary, but we want to be able to quickly access the theme without having to import the whole state (`importFromLocalStorage()`). App theme is semantically separate from editor theme anyway.

body:
The `LinearElementEditor.pointerDownState.segmentMidpoint` should be part of global `pointerDownState`, or possibly the whole `LinearElementEditor.pointerDownState` should be. Because if we're updating `LinearElementEditor.pointerDownState` in a way that requires it to be saved back to `appState` (instead of mutating it), there's a race condition unless we `flushSync` it everywhere we update the linear editor — but even then I'm sure it'd be error prone.

The whole flow we're handling state during pointerdown/pointermove is super brittle and subject to race conditions right now. This manifests in things like:

![excal-midpoint-creation-loop](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/5153846/204149460-2bdd463c-5bf3-48e2-a68b-628427330f82.gif)

Wrapping these calls into `flushSync` seems to fix it, but there are many other places where we do not set (linear editor) state synchronously, so those places may be subject to race conditions as well.
author: dwelle

body:
No, let's not split/rewrite our files just to satisfy tests.

One hack is to reimport the file inside itself:

```
// src/element/textElement.ts
import * as thisModule from "./textElement";
```

and call the `measureText` using `thisModule.measureText()`.

But this might constitute a circular dependency which will disallow later.

---

Also, completely mocking out the implementation of a function (or its significant part) kinda defeats the purpose of the test to begin with, no?

Rather, we can mock the DOM API we're using for dimension calculation that's not working in jest.
author: dwelle

body: Mocking DOM api is unnecessary here since that's not something we need to test, we trust the DOM API. what is needed here is `measureText` returning some measurements instead of 0/1 values and which should change on different text/font etc, hence I was mocking only that part.
